_doc: Book references per CCNA objective. Drawer reads { volume, chapter, page, title, paragraphs[] }. Paragraphs are author-original summaries, NOT excerpts from copyrighted books.

1.1.title: Routers vs switches vs hubs — what each device does
paragraphs: Routers are Layer-3 devices that forward packets between subnets using IP destination addresses. Each interface terminates one subnet and a packet leaving an interface is fully rewritten at Layer 2.
Switches are Layer-2 devices that forward Ethernet frames within a single broadcast domain using MAC addresses. They learn MAC-to-port mappings by inspecting source MACs of incoming frames.

Hubs are Layer-1 repeaters — they retransmit electrical signals on every port. Treat them as a shared collision domain. Hubs do not appear on production CCNA networks but appear on the exam in legacy questions.

1.1.chapter: Ch. 17 — IP Routing in the LAN
1.1.title: Forwarding decision — longest prefix match + administrative distance
paragraphs: When a packet arrives, the router compares the destination IP against every route in the table and picks the route with the longest matching prefix. /30 wins over /24, /24 wins over /16. More specific = always wins.
Administrative distance only breaks ties when two routes have the same prefix length but come from different sources. Connected = 0, static = 1, EIGRP = 90, OSPF = 110, RIP = 120. Lower wins.
Metric breaks ties when routes come from the same source (e.g., two OSPF routes to the same prefix). The protocol's own cost calculation decides.
